lesson.menu_desc: Telling Stories That Connect — sequencing & time markers — 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 14 — Talking About Past Experiences: Telling Stories That Connect and Engage
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Vanessa Giuriati | Aula 14 — Past Stories

listenings.text: Let me tell you about a trip I will never forget. A few years ago, in 2019, I traveled to the south of Italy with two friends. At first, nothing went as planned. We missed our train, and then it started to rain, so we ended up in a tiny village we had never heard of. Honestly, I was upset for about an hour. But that village turned out to be the best part of the whole journey. An old man invited us into his home, his wife cooked for us, and we talked for the entire evening, half in English and half with our hands. Suddenly the rain did not matter anymore. When I look back on that trip now, I do not remember the museums or the famous sights. I remember that one evening, that one moment of kindness from strangers. Eventually we reached the cities we had planned to see, but nothing compared to that small village. That is the funny thing about stories. The best moment is almost never the one you planned.

Here is something I have learned about telling stories. People do not really connect through facts. They connect through moments. If I tell you I went to Italy in 2019, you forget it in a second. But if I tell you how we got lost, how the rain ruined our plan, and how a stranger turned a bad day into an unforgettable one, you remember it. The trick is simple. Do not list everything. Choose one moment that mattered, and build your story around it. Start by setting the scene. A few years ago, when I was younger, last summer. Then take the listener through what happened, step by step. At first this, then that, and in the end something changed. Use the past simple for the actions, and let the small details do the work. A good story is not about how much happened. It is about one thing that suddenly meant something. That is how a simple memory ends up connecting two people who have only just met.

extra_audio.text: First, I set the scene and say a few years ago, in 2019, I traveled to Italy. Then I explain that at first nothing went as planned and we missed our train. After that, I describe the moment when we ended up in a tiny village in the rain. Suddenly, a kind stranger invited us in and the evening turned out to be unforgettable. In the end, when I look back, that one moment is the part of the journey I always remember.
